stories.headline: Artemis II Crew Sets New Human Distance Record After Historic Moon Flyby
stories.hook: Four astronauts just flew farther from Earth than any humans in over 50 years — and they're still on their way home.
stories.body:
On April 6, the crew of NASA's Artemis II mission reached a maximum distance of 252,757 miles from Earth, surpassing the record set by Apollo 13 in 1970 and marking the farthest any humans have traveled since the Apollo era. The four-person crew — NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, and Christina Koch, along with Canadian Space Agency astronaut Jeremy Hansen — became the first people to witness Earthrise over the Moon's far side with their own eyes.

The mission, launched April 1, sent the Orion spacecraft on a 10-day figure-eight trajectory around the Moon without landing. During the lunar flyby, Orion swooped to within 4,067 miles of the lunar surface. The crew spent time demonstrating their spacesuits, performing manual piloting tests, and capturing breathtaking views of both Earth and the Moon's mysterious far side — terrain that has never before been seen in person by any human.

The crew is now heading home and scheduled for splashdown off the coast of San Diego on April 10. Artemis II is the critical rehearsal before Artemis III, which aims to land astronauts — including the first woman and first person of color — on the lunar surface, probably in 2027.

image.alt: Apollo 17 astronauts' view of the lunar surface in the Taurus-Littrow valley
image.credit: Photo by NASA Hubble Space Telescope on Unsplash

links.title: NASA Artemis II Flight Day 7 Update
Artemis II Eclipses Record for Farthest Human Spaceflight
Artemis II lunar flyby highlights — CNN

stories.headline: The World's First Working Quantum Battery Charges With a Laser Beam
stories.hook: Australian scientists just built a battery that defies every rule you know about energy storage.
stories.body:
A team of researchers from CSIRO, RMIT University, and the University of Melbourne has built the world's first proof-of-concept quantum battery — a device that stores and releases energy using the strange rules of quantum physics rather than conventional chemistry. The prototype is a small layered organic device that can be charged wirelessly using a laser, and the results, published in Light: Science & Applications, suggest quantum batteries could one day charge far faster than anything available today.

The key breakthrough is a phenomenon called "super absorption": the battery absorbs laser energy in a rapid quantum event, much faster than a conventional charge cycle. Even more counterintuitive, the team found that quantum batteries charge faster as they get larger — the exact opposite of how today's lithium-ion batteries behave, where scaling up means slower charging times.

There's still a long road ahead. Current prototypes store energy for only microseconds to hours before quantum decoherence — interference from the environment — causes energy to dissipate. But the researchers are optimistic. If the storage-time problem is solved, quantum batteries could eventually power everything from implantable medical devices to electric vehicles, charging in the time it takes to blink.

image.alt: Abstract blue and black glowing light representing quantum energy

links.title: Scientists built a quantum battery that breaks the rules of charging — ScienceDaily
CSIRO quantum battery breakthrough
Prototype Quantum Battery Charges In Seconds, Lasts For Weeks

stories.headline: New AI System Uses 100 Times Less Energy While Getting Better Answers
stories.hook: AI's energy crisis may have a surprising fix: teaching machines to think more like humans.
stories.body:
AI is devouring electricity at an alarming rate — the International Energy Agency estimates U.S. data centers already consume over 10% of the country's electricity, and that figure is set to double by 2030. But a team at Tufts University may have found a way out. Their new "neuro-symbolic" AI system, which blends traditional neural networks with symbolic logic, uses only 1% of the energy required to train a standard AI model — while dramatically outperforming it.

In tests using the classic Tower of Hanoi puzzle, the neuro-symbolic system succeeded 95% of the time. A standard vision-language AI model managed just 34%. On a harder version of the puzzle the robot had never seen before, the neuro-symbolic system still succeeded 78% of the time — the standard AI failed every single attempt. Training the new system took 34 minutes; the old approach took over a day and a half.

The secret is in the architecture. Rather than brute-forcing solutions through billions of pattern-matching calculations, the system uses logical rules to reason about the task — similar to how a human would think through a problem step by step. The research will be presented at the International Conference of Robotics and Automation in Vienna in May. If it scales up, it could reshape both the economics and the carbon footprint of AI.

image.alt: 3D render of an AI processor chip with the letter A on top
image.credit: Photo by Igor Omilaev on Unsplash

links.title: AI breakthrough cuts energy use by 100x while boosting accuracy — ScienceDaily
100x Less Power: The Breakthrough That Could Solve AI's Massive Energy Crisis

stories.headline: 550-Million-Year-Old 'Lost World' in China Pushes Back Animal Evolution by 4 Million Years
stories.hook: Fossils buried in southwest China just rewrote the first chapter of animal life on Earth.
stories.body:
A remarkable fossil site in Yunnan Province, China, has yielded over 700 specimens from the late Ediacaran period — a time before the famous Cambrian explosion that scientists once thought preceded complex animal life. The Jiangchuan Biota, as the site is called, dates to between 554 and 539 million years ago and contains organisms that look startlingly familiar: early relatives of starfish, acorn worms, and even the ancestors of vertebrates like fish and humans.

What makes these fossils extraordinary is their preservation. Unlike typical Ediacaran fossils, which survive only as flat impressions in rock, these specimens are preserved as carbonaceous films — similar to world-famous Cambrian sites like Canada's Burgess Shale. That level of detail has allowed researchers from Oxford University and Yunnan University to identify feeding structures, digestive systems, and body plans in specimens that are 4 million years older than anything comparable.

The discovery, published in Science, directly challenges the conventional narrative that complex animal life exploded onto the scene during the Cambrian period roughly 538 million years ago. Instead, it suggests the roots of the animal kingdom were already well established before that explosion even began. "This closes a major gap in the earliest phases of animal diversification," said one of the researchers involved.

image.alt: Fossilized fish skeleton embedded in blue rock, showing prehistoric preservation
image.credit: Photo by Kilian Murphy on Unsplash

links.title: Scientists found a 'lost world' of animals that shouldn't exist yet — ScienceDaily
2026 in science — Wikipedia

stories.headline: Scientists Found the Protein That Ages Your Brain — and Reversed It in Mice
stories.hook: One protein. When researchers blocked it in old mice, their memories improved.
stories.body:
Researchers at UC San Francisco have identified a single protein — called FTL1 (Ferritin Light Chain 1) — as a key driver of brain aging. In older mice, FTL1 accumulates in the hippocampus, the brain region responsible for learning and memory, weakening connections between neurons and slowing cellular metabolism. The finding, published in Nature Aging, could point toward new treatments for age-related cognitive decline, Alzheimer's disease, and dementia.

The team tracked changes in genes and proteins in mouse hippocampi over a lifetime and found that FTL1 was uniquely and consistently elevated in older animals. When they artificially boosted FTL1 levels in young mice, those animals' brains began to look and function like the brains of old mice. But the reversal experiment was the one that turned heads: when FTL1 was reduced in elderly mice, the connections between their brain cells increased, and their performance on memory tests measurably improved.

The researchers also found that treating cells with compounds that boost metabolism could prevent the negative effects of high FTL1 levels entirely. While the jump from mice to humans is never guaranteed, FTL1 is highly conserved across species, meaning the same mechanism likely exists in human brains. The finding opens up a new class of potential targets for fighting cognitive aging.

image.alt: A brain rendered with glowing blue neural connections representing brain activity
image.credit: Photo by Shubham Dhage on Unsplash

links.title: Scientists found a protein that drives brain aging — ScienceDaily
UCSF Scientists Identify FTL1 Protein Linked to Brain Aging
Targeting FTL1 protein in aging mice improves cognitive impairment — Nature Aging

stories.headline: Mars Is Crackling With Hidden Lightning, and It's Rewriting the Planet's Chemistry
stories.hook: Mars doesn't just have dust storms — it has electric storms powerful enough to spark glowing discharges.
stories.body:
NASA's Perseverance rover has recorded 55 electrical discharges during Martian dust devils and storm fronts, confirming what scientists had long suspected: Mars crackles with static electricity on a planetary scale. The findings, published this week, show that when Martian dust particles collide in storms, they build up charge that eventually releases in faint, glowing discharges — essentially tiny lightning events. Because the Martian atmosphere is so thin, these discharges ignite more easily than they would on Earth.

But the implications go beyond the spectacle. These electrical events are triggering chemical reactions that reshape Mars's surface and atmosphere in surprising ways. The discharges create a cocktail of highly oxidizing compounds — including chlorates and perchlorates — and leave behind distinct isotopic signatures in the soil. In other words, Martian dust storms aren't just moving dirt around; they're actively manufacturing chemicals across the planet's surface.

For future human missions, the research carries a practical warning. Electrostatic discharges could damage sensitive electronics, interfere with communications equipment, and pose safety hazards for astronauts working in spacesuits. Understanding the scale and frequency of Martian electrical activity is now considered essential groundwork for designing habitats and equipment that can withstand it.

image.alt: Detailed view of the planet Mars against a black background showing craters and reddish terrain

links.title: Mars dust storms are sparking electricity and rewriting the planet's chemistry — ScienceDaily
NASA Rover Detects Electric Sparks in Mars Dust Devils, Storms

stories.headline: Scientists Discover 24 New Deep-Sea Species — Including an Entirely New Branch of Life
stories.hook: In a part of the Pacific earmarked for mining, researchers just found creatures so strange they had to invent a new category for them.
stories.body:
Scientists exploring the Clarion-Clipperton Zone (CCZ) — a stretch of Pacific seafloor between Hawaii and Mexico that governments are actively preparing to mine for battery metals — have discovered 24 new species of amphipods, plus something entirely unexpected: a new superfamily of deep-sea creatures called Mirabestioidea that represents a branch of life that had never been identified before. It has conical mouthparts unlike anything seen in its group, and it's not even rare: researchers found more than 25 specimens, suggesting it's a common resident of the habitat.

Amphipods are small crustaceans that act as janitors of the deep seafloor. They scavenge carrion, hunt smaller prey, and in turn feed larger animals, moving energy through an ecosystem where food is scarce. The newly discovered species were collected during expeditions studying the ecological baseline of the CCZ, where mineral-rich nodules on the seafloor attract interest from mining companies across multiple nations.

The timing of the discovery is pointed. International regulations governing deep-sea mining remain incomplete, and the new findings underscore just how much remains unknown about the ecosystems that would be disrupted. "Mining might be allowed to occur before scientists even have the chance to name species that call the seabed home," researchers warned. The study was published alongside an urgent call for tougher environmental protections.

image.alt: Close-up of a deep-sea lantern fish glowing in the dark water
image.credit: Photo by Noé Sardet on Unsplash

links.title: 24 new deep-sea species found including a rare new branch of life — ScienceDaily
Scientists Found an Entirely New Branch of Deep-Sea Creatures Right Where the US Government Wants to Mine
Deep-sea research reveals entirely new branch of life

stories.headline: A 23-Million-Year-Old Rhino Once Roamed the Canadian High Arctic
stories.hook: Scientists just found a nearly complete rhino skeleton in Nunavut — one of the last places you'd expect one.
stories.body:
Paleontologists have announced the discovery of a new rhinoceros species from the Canadian High Arctic, in ancient lakebed sediments at Haughton Crater on Devon Island, Nunavut — about 1,000 kilometers north of the Arctic Circle. Named Epiaceratherium itjilik (the "itjilik" meaning "frost" in Inuktitut, chosen after consultation with Inuit Elders), the species lived roughly 23 million years ago during the Early Miocene, when the region was covered in temperate forests rather than tundra. About 75% of the skeleton was recovered, making it one of the most complete fossil rhino specimens ever found.

E. itjilik was smaller and slimmer than modern African rhinos, more resembling the Indian rhino in body shape, and had no horn. Its presence in the High Arctic suggests the North Atlantic region played a far larger role in the evolution and migration of rhinoceroses than scientists previously realized. The animal likely crossed land bridges that connected what is now Canada to Europe and Asia during a warmer period when sea levels were lower.

The study, published in Nature Ecology and Evolution, adds to a growing body of evidence that the Arctic was once a critical corridor for large mammal evolution. The researchers believe "frosty rhinos" may have spread northward during warm periods and retreated south during cooler epochs, leaving behind scattered fossils as breadcrumbs of their ancient migrations.

image.alt: Rugged white and brown mountains of Auyuittuq National Park in the Canadian Arctic
image.credit: Photo by Isaac Demeester on Unsplash

links.title: Scientists found a rhino in the Arctic and it changes everything — ScienceDaily
23-million-year-old 'frosty rhino' discovered in the High Arctic
New fossil rhino species lived 1,000 km north of Arctic Circle — CBC

stories.headline: 12,000-Year-Old Dice Found in the Americas Rewrites the History of Gambling
stories.hook: Ice Age hunter-gatherers on the American Great Plains were rolling dice 6,000 years before anyone else in the world.
stories.body:
A new study published in the journal American Antiquity has upended the history of games of chance. Researchers analyzing archaeological collections found that Native American hunter-gatherers were crafting and using dice-like objects — carefully shaped bone "binary lots" — over 12,000 years ago at Folsom-period sites in Wyoming, Colorado, and New Mexico. That's more than 6,000 years before comparable dice appear in the archaeological record in the Old World.

The objects were small, flat or slightly curved bone pieces designed to be held in the hand, with two clearly distinct sides created through markings, surface treatments, or color differences — functionally similar to a coin flip, but deliberately made to randomize outcomes in structured games. Evidence of these tools now spans 57 archaeological sites across 12 U.S. states, from the Paleoindian period through the Late Prehistoric, suggesting games of chance were a persistent and widespread cultural practice.

Why would people living in the Ice Age invest time making gambling implements? The researchers argue that games of chance created neutral, rule-governed spaces for people from different groups to interact, exchange goods, form alliances, and resolve tensions without violence. Gambling, in other words, may have been one of humanity's earliest diplomatic tools — and Americans were playing the game longer than anyone.

image.alt: Ancient Egyptian dice from the Roman Period in the Birmingham Museum collection
image.credit: Photo by Birmingham Museums Trust on Unsplash

links.title: 12,000-Year-Old Native American Dice Rewrite the History of Gambling — SciTechDaily
Ancient bone dice reveal 12,000-year history of gambling — ScienceDaily
Native Americans had dice and games of probability long before other cultures — NBC News

stories.headline: Physicists Trapped Light in a Layer 1,000 Times Thinner Than a Human Hair
stories.hook: An impossibly thin sheet of material just did something that shouldn't be possible.
stories.body:
Researchers from the University of Warsaw and collaborators have created a nanoscale structure that traps infrared light in a layer just 40 nanometers thick — over 1,000 times thinner than a human hair, and thinner than most viruses. The key is molybdenum diselenide (MoSe₂), a material with an extraordinarily high refractive index of about 4.5. Light slows down dramatically inside it — far more than it does in glass (1.5) or even silicon (3.5) — allowing it to be confined in layers that would be far too thin for conventional photonics.

The trapped light also does something unexpected: when confined in this ultra-thin layer, the structure converts infrared radiation into visible blue light at a rate 1,500 times stronger than a flat layer of the same material. This enormous amplification of light conversion effects could be useful for sensors, solar energy collectors, and next-generation communication devices that operate at the nanoscale.

Crucially, the team used molecular beam epitaxy (MBE) — an existing, scalable semiconductor manufacturing technique — to grow large, uniform MoSe₂ films spanning several square inches. That practical production method moves this discovery much closer to real-world applications than most nanophotonics breakthroughs. Smaller, faster, more efficient optical chips could be on the horizon.

image.alt: A red and white laser beam refracting through a crystal, splitting into colorful spectra
image.credit: Photo by Daniele Levis Pelusi on Unsplash

links.title: Scientists trap light in a layer 1,000x thinner than hair — ScienceDaily
Ultra-thin MoSe₂ grating traps infrared light in a 40-nanometer layer — Phys.org

stories.headline: Ancient Bees Made Their Nests Inside Fossil Bones in a Caribbean Cave
stories.hook: A chain of bizarre events in a cave on Hispaniola created a discovery that had never been seen before in the fossil record.
stories.body:
Paleontologists working in Cueva de Mono, a cave in the southern Dominican Republic, have uncovered an extraordinary scene frozen in time: ancient burrowing bees that made their nests inside the fossilized bones of extinct animals. The find — published in April 2026 and described as unlike anything previously known — reveals that thousands of years ago, giant barn owls repeatedly dropped prey into this cave, accumulating the bones of extinct hutias and ground sloths in sediment-rich chambers.

At some point, burrowing bees moved in. Finding soft, pliable sediment perfect for their nests, the bees did something no bee species has ever been documented doing: they excavated their brood cells directly inside the tooth sockets and vertebrae of the fossilized bones. Inside each hollow, they laid their eggs and lined the chambers with a smooth, waterproof organic coating — and then were themselves preserved alongside their hosts.

It's only the second recorded case of burrowing bees nesting inside a cave, and the very first time any bee species has been found using pre-existing fossil structures as ready-made burrows. The identity of the bee species remains unknown; only the lining of its nests survived. The discovery is a reminder of just how strange and contingent the history of life can be — and how much remains hidden in the world's overlooked caves.

image.alt: A brown and black bee resting on a rough brown surface
image.credit: Photo by Ricardo Gomez Angel on Unsplash

links.title: Ancient bees found nesting inside fossil bones in rare cave discovery — ScienceDaily
Ancient bees burrowed inside bones, fossils reveal — Scientific American
These fossils were the perfect home for ancient baby bees — Field Museum

stories.headline: The Silicon-Carbon Battery Revolution Is Finally Coming to Your Smartphone
stories.hook: The next wave of phones will last all day — and recharge in minutes.
stories.body:
After years of incremental improvements to lithium-ion batteries, a new generation of silicon-carbon (Si/C) batteries is beginning to reach mainstream smartphones — and the jump in performance is significant. Unlike conventional batteries that use graphite anodes, silicon-carbon anodes can hold far more lithium ions, translating to energy densities 40–50% higher than current flagship phones. Several major Chinese manufacturers have already shipped devices with the technology, and in 2026 it is moving upmarket into flagship devices globally.

Honor's Magic V6, announced at MWC 2026, packs a 7,150 mAh silicon-carbon battery — more than double the capacity of typical phones a decade ago — while remaining competitive in weight. Xiaomi's flagship sports a 6,000 mAh Si/C battery. The phones can handle faster charging rates, meaning filling from empty to full in under 30 minutes is becoming routine. Apple and Samsung, who have lagged in Si/C adoption due to manufacturing complexity, are now expected to begin the transition in their next major hardware cycles.

The catch: silicon expands as it absorbs lithium ions, creating stress fractures over time that degrade battery capacity. Manufacturers are addressing this by blending silicon with carbon to buffer the expansion. The tradeoff is lower silicon content — and therefore more modest performance gains — but the consensus is that the engineering challenge is now solvable at scale. The era of genuinely all-day smartphones may finally be arriving.

image.alt: A hand holding a smartphone connected to a fast-charging cable
image.credit: Photo by Onur Binay on Unsplash

links.title: Silicon Carbon Battery Smartphones: The Real 2026 Story
Should you buy a phone with a silicon-carbon battery? Here's the catch — Android Authority

stories.headline: James Webb Telescope Found a 'Forbidden' Planet That Shouldn't Exist
stories.hook: TOI-5205 b is a Jupiter-sized world orbiting a star so small it shouldn't be able to form such a planet — and its atmosphere makes even less sense.
stories.body:
The James Webb Space Telescope has delivered a puzzling portrait of TOI-5205 b, a Jupiter-sized exoplanet orbiting a red dwarf star barely 40% the mass of our Sun. According to established planet formation models, a star this small shouldn't have enough material to build a gas giant — which is why astronomers nicknamed it a "forbidden planet" when it was first discovered. But Webb's new observations, published in The Astronomical Journal, reveal that the mystery runs even deeper.

Webb detected methane and hydrogen sulfide in the planet's atmosphere — and found that the atmosphere is startlingly poor in heavy elements. That's unexpected for a gas giant around a small star; theory predicts such planets should be rich in metals (everything heavier than hydrogen and helium, in astronomical parlance). Instead, TOI-5205 b's atmosphere appears far less enriched than even its host star, which itself has relatively few heavy elements. One hypothesis is that the planet's interior didn't mix with its atmosphere during formation, leaving heavy elements locked deep inside.

The discovery was made possible by Webb's ability to capture multiple transits — passages of the planet across the face of its star — and analyze the precise chemical fingerprints left in the light. The implications are significant: if planets like this can form and persist around small red dwarf stars (the most common type of star in the galaxy), our models of how solar systems assemble need substantial revision.

image.alt: A planet with green glowing continents against the dark of deep space
image.credit: Photo by Bhautik Patel on Unsplash

links.title: This 'forbidden' exoplanet has an atmosphere scientists can't explain — ScienceDaily
Giant 'forbidden planet' orbiting small star shows an unusually low-metal atmosphere — Phys.org
Astronomers Found Something Strange In Giant 'Forbidden' Planet — Futurism

stories.headline: Baby Dinosaur Found in South Korea Gets Named After a Beloved Cartoon Character
stories.hook: Scientists unearthed a rare baby dinosaur fossil — then gave it a name that will make any '80s kid smile.
stories.body:
Paleontologists in South Korea have announced the discovery of a remarkably rare specimen: a baby dinosaur fossil in excellent condition, now officially named Doolysaurus after the beloved Australian animated character Dooley from the 1980s cartoon series. The unusual naming reflects a growing trend of scientists using popular culture references to bring public attention to new species, particularly when the creature has qualities that feel endearingly small and charming. The tiny fossil fills an important gap in understanding how certain dinosaur lineages grew from hatchling to adult.

Baby dinosaur fossils are exceptionally scarce in the paleontological record, because the bones of hatchlings and juveniles are fragile and rarely survive the millions of years needed to become fossils. When they do survive, they often belong to species already known from adult specimens — allowing scientists to compare body proportions, bone structure, and growth rates at different life stages. Doolysaurus provides exactly this kind of window into the growth of a previously known South Korean dinosaur lineage.

The find is part of a broader surge in Korean paleontology, which has seen significant investment in fossil prospecting over the past decade. The country's Late Cretaceous fossil beds, particularly in the South Jeolla and Gyeongsang provinces, have yielded important finds including dinosaur eggs and trackways. Doolysaurus joins a growing list of Korean dinosaurs, with its cartoon-inspired name likely to make it one of the more memorable.

image.alt: Dinosaur skeleton displayed in a museum exhibit under dramatic lighting
image.credit: Photo by Zhongyi Chen on Unsplash

links.title: Scientists uncovered a rare baby dinosaur in South Korea — Archaeology Magazine
2026 in archaeology — Wikipedia

stories.headline: A Greek Inscription Hidden Under a Syrian Mosque May Reveal a Lost Sun Temple
stories.hook: Beneath a 1,400-year-old mosque in Syria, a few lines of ancient Greek could end one of classical history's longest-running debates.
stories.body:
Archaeologists have discovered a mysterious Greek inscription beneath the Great Mosque of Homs in central Syria — a find that could finally pinpoint the location of an ancient sun temple whose precise whereabouts have been disputed by scholars for centuries. The temple in question is believed to be the famous sanctuary of the sun god Elagabal, a deity of extraordinary importance in the ancient world: the Roman emperor Elagabalus (218–222 AD) took his very name and identity from this god, and reportedly transported the temple's sacred black stone to Rome at the height of his rule.

Homs — ancient Emesa — was the cult center of Elagabal, and historians have long theorized that Roman and Byzantine religious structures in the city were built over or adjacent to the original sanctuary. The discovery of a Greek inscription beneath the mosque's foundations provides direct epigraphic evidence linking the site to classical antiquity, and researchers are now working to translate and analyze the text in full. Early readings suggest it dates to the Roman imperial period and may include a dedicatory formula.

If confirmed, the finding would represent one of the most significant classical archaeology discoveries in the Middle East in years — a direct material connection between a site of active Islamic worship and the pagan Roman religious landscape that preceded it by nearly two millennia. The full details of the inscription are expected to be published in an international archaeology journal later this year.

image.alt: Ancient white stone columns near the sea at a Greek archaeological site in the Crimea
image.credit: Photo by Konstantin Dyadyun on Unsplash

links.title: March/April 2026 — Archaeology Magazine
2026 in archaeology — Wikipedia
